steps.title: Identify the contrasting viewpoints
steps.content: The passage structures its main idea around an explicit opposition: “Some critics have found fault... Other critics, however, argue that...” This setup introduces two opposing interpretations of how to evaluate the series—one negative, one defensive. The passage's organizing principle is presenting these two conflicting critical perspectives, not celebrating O'Brian's work or judging its merits.

steps.title: Match the purpose to the choices
steps.content: Choice B captures the core: the passage presents critics with differing views about whether the books' structural choices (abrupt endings) succeed or fail. The disagreement hinges on how to interpret the series—as discrete novels or as a single long work—which directly addresses the efficacy (effectiveness) of the structure. The other three choices miss the mark: A misframes this as O'Brian's intentional design choice rather than critics' debate, C confuses the comparison (the Forsyte Saga is an analogy for how to view the series, not a standard of renown), and D invents praise for entertainment that the passage never offers. That matches choice B — B is correct.
